name: SlateDB
description: SlateDB is a cloud-native embedded key-value storage engine built on object storage (S3, MinIO, local filesystem). It implements an LSM-tree architecture that writes data directly to object storage, eliminating the need for local disks while providing 99.999999999% durability. SlateDB supports a single-writer, multi-reader deployment model with a formally verified manifest fencing protocol. It provides transactional semantics with snapshots and optimistic concurrency control, zero-copy clones and branches, and tunable write durability vs. latency trade-offs. The Rust core library ships with official UniFFI-based bindings for Python, Go, Java, and Node.js.
format_on_disk: LSM-Tree (SST files + WAL written to object storage)
core_strengths: Zero-disk architecture: persists directly to S3/MinIO/local object storage
LSM-tree engine with formally verified manifest fencing protocol
Single-writer, multi-reader deployment with OCC transactions and snapshots
Zero-copy clones and branches for O(1) database forking
Tunable write durability vs. latency for cost-sensitive workloads
Official bindings for Python, Go, Java, and Node.js via UniFFI
best_use_cases: Cloud-native services that need embedded KV storage without local disks
Stream processors, caches, and feature stores built on object storage
Databases and workflow engines using object storage as the persistence layer
Serverless and containerized workloads where local disk is ephemeral
remarks: SlateDB writes directly to object storage; a local disk cache is used only to accelerate reads. Write durability guarantees depend on the object store's consistency model — S3 and MinIO provide strong consistency, but in-memory stores are best-effort. The single-writer constraint means only one process may write at a time; the manifest fencing protocol prevents split-brain but does not support concurrent writes. Compaction can run embedded or as a separate process. The Python package (pip install slatedb) is a UniFFI binding around the Rust core, requiring Python 3.10+. The project joined the Commonhaus Foundation in 2025.
